title: How to scope a custom web application | Bc. Ondřej Halata (halatao.cz)

seo.title: How to scope a custom web application | Bc. Ondřej Halata (halatao.cz)
seo.description: A practical guide to scoping a custom web application without overloading the first phase or starting with vague expectations.

page.title: How to scope a custom web application | Bc. Ondřej Halata (halatao.cz)
page.h1: How to scope a custom web application without creating chaos
page.description: A practical guide to scoping a custom web application without overloading the first phase or starting with vague expectations.
page.primaryQuery: how to scope a custom web application

hero.title: A good project brief is not a novel
hero.subtitle: The goal is not to describe every button. The goal is to define the problem, outcome, roles, constraints, and first delivery phase clearly enough to move.

intro: Many teams delay a project because they do not have a perfect specification. In practice, a solid frame matters more than a huge document.
If the problem, desired outcome, and first-phase boundary are clear, the project can start with much less risk and much more useful feedback.

sections.title: Recommended approach
body: A good outcome rarely starts with an endless specification. It starts with a practical frame that can be tested in the first delivery phase.
bullets: define the business problem and expected value
describe the main roles and workflow
decide what the first release must solve

sections.title: Common mistakes
body: The biggest problem is usually not a lack of specification text. It is weak prioritisation and an unclear goal for the first phase.
bullets: mixing nice-to-have ideas into the first phase
no owner on the client side
thinking about screens before the process

sections.title: What the outcome should be
body: The guide should help you make a better project decision, not add another document with no delivery impact.
bullets: lower risk of solving secondary problems first

faq.question: Do we need wireframes first?
faq.answer: Not always. Process, roles, and desired outcome usually matter more at the start than detailed visuals.

faq.question: How much detail is enough?
faq.answer: Enough to shape the first phase and identify risk. Not so much that planning becomes a project in itself.

faq.question: Is a workshop useful before implementation?
faq.answer: Often yes, especially when several roles or teams need alignment on the workflow and priorities.

for: lower risk of solving secondary problems first

props.heading: A good project brief is not a novel
props.body: The goal is not to describe every button. The goal is to define the problem, outcome, roles, constraints, and first delivery phase clearly enough to move.

Many teams delay a project because they do not have a perfect specification. In practice, a solid frame matters more than a huge document.

If the problem, desired outcome, and first-phase boundary are clear, the project can start with much less risk and much more useful feedback.
